abstract: This paper tests whether Vision-and-Large-Language-Models (VLLMs) can improve semantic image clustering for computational social science by generating connotative textual descriptions of images that are then embedded and clustered. Using a dataset of 11,873 Instagram images related to climate change, the authors compare a VLLM-based pipeline (GPT-4-turbo, with LLaVA as an open alternative) against a CNN-based pipeline (VGG16), evaluating both denotative and connotative cluster quality as well as interpretability. They find that the VLLM-based approach substantially improves connotative clustering quality and offers high interpretability via TF-IDF keyword summaries of clusters, at a small cost to denotative coherence.
key_claims: Semantic clustering for social science should be framed as connotative clustering—grouping images by socially and culturally embedded meaning rather than by depicted objects.
CNN-based clustering pipelines, currently dominant in computational social science, capture denotative content well but miss connotative meaning and lack interpretability.
VLLMs can generate textual descriptions that capture connotative meaning of images (including memes and culturally complex visuals), enabling text-based clustering pipelines.
A VLLM-to-text-to-embedding-to-clustering pipeline produces significantly higher connotative cluster quality than a CNN-based pipeline, with only a small loss in denotative quality.
The intermediate textual representations make clusters human-interpretable via TF-IDF keyword summaries, addressing a key limitation of CNN-based approaches.

The improvement holds (though at lower absolute quality) when using an open-source VLLM (LLaVA) instead of a proprietary one (GPT-4-turbo), suggesting the finding is about the class of model rather than a specific implementation.
methods: Dataset: 11,873 Instagram images related to climate change (Zhang & Peng, 2022a), previously used for clustering benchmarks.

VLLM-based pipeline: generate one-paragraph connotative descriptions via GPT-4-turbo (and LLaVA-1.5-7b-hf for open replication), embed text with BERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2), reduce dimensions, and cluster.
CNN-based baseline pipeline: VGG16 feature extraction, dimensionality reduction, and clustering.
Dimensionality reduction: UMAP (5/10 components; 5/15/30 nearest neighbors) and PCA (5/10/20 components, plus 80% variance).
Clustering: HDBSCAN with minimum cluster sizes of 50, 100, and 200, allowing outliers.

Evaluation of cluster quality: adaptation of Grimmer & King (2011) cluster quality measure, splitting similarity into denotative and connotative dimensions; 500 image pairs rated by expert human coders on a 3-point scale, with two consensus rounds (Krippendorff's α improving from .55/.56 to .81/.71).

Interpretability evaluation: TF-IDF keyword summaries generated per cluster; three human evaluators matched 160 random image sets (3 images each) to cluster descriptions, with inter-rater Cohen's Kappa averaging .74.
Robustness checks: alternative embedding models (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small), alternative CNNs, and alternative prompts reported in the appendix.
findings: VLLM-based clustering achieves substantially higher connotative quality scores than CNN-based clustering across all tested minimum cluster sizes.
CNN-based clustering achieves marginally higher denotative quality, reflecting an expected trade-off when prioritizing connotative meaning.
Larger minimum cluster sizes increase variance and can degrade connotative quality for the VLLM pipeline by merging connotatively distinct but visually similar images.

Qualitative comparison shows the VLLM pipeline clusters wind turbines and solar panels together (connotative coherence: renewable energy), while the CNN pipeline groups visually similar Earth imagery despite semantic heterogeneity.

Open-source LLaVA replicates the trend of VLLM-based connotative gains over CNNs but with lower absolute scores; LLaVA was less accurate at reading text and missed some symbolic cues (e.g., a runic eco-fascist symbol identified by GPT-4).

Interpretability test: average precision and recall of .83 (accuracy .83) for matching image sets to TF-IDF cluster descriptions, versus ~.03 expected by chance; rising to .87 after merging semantically adjacent clusters.
18 of 32 clusters achieved perfect precision and 19 perfect recall, with errors concentrated on semantically overlapping cluster pairs (e.g., nature/water variants, protest/youth-activism).
contributions: Reframes semantic clustering for social science explicitly as connotative clustering, drawing on Barthes' denotation/connotation distinction.
Proposes and evaluates a VLLM-to-text image clustering pipeline as a drop-in alternative to CNN feature extraction, enabling direct comparison.
Introduces an adaptation of the Grimmer & King cluster quality measure that separately quantifies denotative and connotative validity using human-annotated image pairs.
Provides empirical evidence that VLLM-based pipelines deliver both higher connotative quality and substantially improved interpretability for visual clustering in social science contexts.
Demonstrates the approach using a publicly available climate-communication image dataset, enabling comparison with prior benchmarks (Zhang & Peng, 2024).
Identifies trade-offs, biases, and future directions—including prompt engineering, topic modeling on VLLM descriptions, and handling outliers—for VLLM-based visual analysis.
framing: The paper positions itself within computational social science and visual political communication, responding to the 'visual turn' in social media research and the limitations of CNN-based image clustering for studying meaning-laden visual content like memes and protest imagery. It engages with prior semantic clustering literature (SIC, BoVP, CLIP-based methods) and recent computer-science work on text-guided clustering (Stephan et al., 2024), but differentiates itself by foregrounding connotative meaning, social-science interpretability, and Barthesian semiotic theory rather than benchmark-driven object recognition.
